commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: - [P2] Maintainers need to choose the canonical memory FTS strategy and upgrade shape; there is no narrow automated repair left on this branch.; Cleared: No concrete security or supply-chain concern was found; the live PR files change local memory indexing/schema/tests and add no dependencies, workflows, downloads, permissions, or secret handling. (sha=6e0e06e3acb700f73baca8d93752e019b586d372)
commands.review_summary:
This PR adds a separate path FTS table for memory chunks, keeps body FTS separate, repairs and publishes derived FTS rows from canonical `memory_index_chunks`, and merges body/path keyword candidates before limiting results.

PR surface: Source +260, Tests +798. Total +1058 across 10 files.

Reproducibility: yes. source-reproducible. Current main and v2026.6.8 store memory paths as `UNINDEXED` FTS metadata while writing only chunk body text into the indexed FTS column, matching the linked filename/date-token miss.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] Maintainers need to choose the canonical memory FTS strategy and upgrade shape; there is no narrow automated repair left on this branch.

target.title: fix(memory-core): include path in FTS indexed text so filename tokens are searchable
target.body:
## What problem does this PR solve?

`openclaw memory search` can rank filename/date-token queries poorly or miss them when the filename token only lives in the derived path FTS index. The current PR was also stale against the canonical `memory_index_*` table migration, so the original fix needed to be rebased onto the current schema owner instead of keeping the old `chunks_*` wiring.

## Why does this matter now?

The linked issue is a user-visible memory lookup failure: a clean memory index and working content search still did not reliably return the expected memory file for a filename/date-token query such as `2026-06-17-1649`. Without this repair, users can have the right memory file indexed but still fail to retrieve it by the filename/date token they know.

## What is the intended outcome?

Filename/date-token keyword searches should find the matching memory file while body-text BM25 ranking remains based on body text only. `memory_index_chunks` remains canonical, and both derived FTS tables are repaired or published from that canonical table.

## What is intentionally out of scope?

This does not change memory file format, vector search scoring, providers, auth, network behavior, gateway startup behavior, or the public memory API contract.

## Summary

- Fix classification: root-cause compatibility and reliability repair for memory FTS path search on the current canonical memory index schema.
- Maintainer-ready confidence: high for the local memory code path because current head `6e0e06e3acb7` has CLI proof for the filename/date-token lookup, passes the focused memory schema/search regression suite, passes the extension test typecheck, and has a final ClawSweeper commit review result of `nothing_found`.
- Root cause: filename/date-token matches live in a derived path FTS index, but search/schema/publish paths were not consistently treating `memory_index_chunks` as the source of truth for both `memory_index_chunks_fts` and `memory_index_chunks_fts_path`. The repair path also needed to rebuild derived FTS rows atomically so an interrupted upgrade cannot drop previously usable lexical rows.
- What changed: memory indexing now writes both the body FTS table and the separate path FTS table; stale cleanup removes both derived FTS rows; schema repair rebuilds missing or unmarked derived FTS rows from `memory_index_chunks` inside a savepoint with set-based SQL; full shadow reindex publishing copies `memory_index_chunks_fts_path`; keyword search merges body/path rows, keeps the best rank per chunk id, sorts the merged rows, and only then applies the final limit.
- Why it matters / User impact: `openclaw memory search "2026-06-17-1649"` can find `memory/2026-06-17-1649.md` by filename/date token without prepending path text into the body index or weakening body BM25 relevance.
- Architecture / source-of-truth check: `memory_index_chunks` remains canonical; FTS tables are derived indexes; schema setup and full reindex publish own derived-index repair; search owns merging body/path keyword candidates before result limiting.
- Patch quality notes: the larger diff is from carrying the existing PR through the current canonical table migration, not from adding an unrelated feature. The repair marker is stored in `memory_index_meta` and keyed by the derived FTS table names, so legacy or unmarked indexes rebuild once while clean current indexes avoid repeated correlated scans over `UNINDEXED` FTS metadata columns.
- Related open PR scan: this updates my existing open PR #94114 for linked issue #94102. Sibling PR #94135 remains a maintainer selection signal; this PR now uses the current canonical `memory_index_*` schema rather than the stale pre-rebase table shape.

## Linked context

Closes #94102

Related #94114

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: memory search should find indexed memory files by filename/date tokens through the memory index/search path while preserving body BM25 ranking and keeping schema repair safe for existing local memory indexes.
- Real environment tested: isolated local OpenClaw CLI memory indexing/search on current head `6e0e06e3acb7`, using a temporary OpenClaw home/workspace and `memorySearch.provider: "none"`. This exercises the local memory indexing/search/schema code path changed by this PR.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

  ```bash
  node ./openclaw.mjs memory index --force
  node ./openclaw.mjs memory search "2026-06-17-1649" --json --max-results 3
  node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test-repro.tsbuildinfo
  OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_LOCK_SCOPE=worktree node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/memory-schema.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-search.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-db.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts
  ```

- Evidence after fix:

  ```text
  HEAD=6e0e06e3acb7
  $ node ./openclaw.mjs memory index --force
  Memory index updated (main).
  $ node ./openclaw.mjs memory search "2026-06-17-1649" --json --max-results 3
  {
    "results": [
      {
        "id": "2fc0e5052cd30a063fe293b5c42bfbf822471f493651c3926068b82dad822d8c",
        "path": "memory/2026-06-17-1649.md",
        "startLine": 1,
        "endLine": 2,
        "score": 0.9000039999839999,
        "textScore": 0.000003999984000064,
        "snippet": "OAuth token notes and Google testing mode details.\n",
        "source": "memory"
      }
    ]
  }

  Validation result: check-test-types-head-final pass, exit_code=0.

  Test Files  4 passed (4)
  Tests  99 passed (99)
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the current head returns `memory/2026-06-17-1649.md` for the CLI filename/date-token query and passes the canonical memory search/schema regressions, including date-token filename lookup, merge-before-limit behavior, unmarked FTS repair from `memory_index_chunks`, rollback preservation when unmarked FTS repair fails, clean-index scan skipping after the repair marker is current, legacy source migration, and shadow path FTS publication.
- This update did not exercise provider auth, external OAuth, gateway session delivery, or network behavior because the changed path is local memory indexing/search/schema repair.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

```bash
node ./openclaw.mjs memory index --force
node ./openclaw.mjs memory search "2026-06-17-1649" --json --max-results 3
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test-repro.tsbuildinfo
OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_LOCK_SCOPE=worktree node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/memory-schema.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-search.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-db.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts
```

Target test files:

- `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/memory-schema.test.ts`
- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-search.test.ts`
- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-db.test.ts`
- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Body/path FTS candidates are merged before the requested result limit is applied, so a filename hit is not dropped when body matches fill the limit.
- `memory_index_chunks_fts` / `memory_index_chunks_fts_path` row-count mismatch or missing repair marker rebuilds derived rows from canonical `memory_index_chunks`.
- Unmarked FTS repair now preserves previous derived FTS rows if the repair fails before completion.
- Clean FTS schema setup skips repeated `WHERE NOT EXISTS` virtual-table drift scans after the repair marker is current.
- Full shadow memory reindex publication copies `memory_index_chunks_fts_path`, so filename/date-token rows survive in-place reindex publish.
- Legacy memory stores without `source` columns migrate into canonical rows with the default `memory` source before rebuilding derived FTS rows.
- Merged keyword rows use `toSorted`, avoiding in-place ordering mutation while preserving rank order.

- Focused regression command after this patch: the four memory manager/schema test files above were run together against the current head and passed 99 tests.
- Scenario locked in: canonical chunk rows remain the source of truth while body and path FTS tables are repaired/published separately, marked after repair, and merged before result limiting.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: these tests exercise the changed search merge/rank behavior, the schema-owner repair path, the repair-marker skip path, the repair rollback path, the legacy migration path, and the full reindex publish path directly without requiring provider embeddings or gateway-only startup proof.

## CI attribution

- Current proof is bound to PR head `6e0e06e3acb7`.
- `check-test-types` was PR-introduced: the new filename-token test passed an unsupported `storePath` field to `createCfg`, causing `TS2353`. Current head removes that invalid test-only field and passes the same extension test typecheck.
- The earlier `checks-node-core-tooling` failure was in `src/scripts/test-projects.test.ts`, outside this PR's memory-core/memory-host-sdk diff. I did not change tooling code for that failure.
- The earlier `checks-node-agentic-agents-core-runtime` failure was in `src/agents/code-mode.test.ts`, outside this PR's memory-core/memory-host-sdk diff. I did not change agent runtime code for that failure.
- Final local pre-submit review: ClawSweeper commit review on `6e0e06e3acb7` reported `nothing_found` with `check_conclusion=success`.
- Fresh remote checks should be evaluated against the current PR head.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes`

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `Yes`

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `No`

What is the highest-risk area?

Existing local memory SQLite stores may have derived FTS tables that are missing rows, use older FTS layouts, or were built before `source` existed on legacy memory tables.

How is that risk mitigated?

The repair treats `memory_index_chunks` as source of truth and rebuilds derived FTS rows when row counts differ or the repair marker is missing. The rebuild runs inside a SQLite savepoint and uses set-based `INSERT ... SELECT`, so a failed repair rolls back to the previous derived FTS rows instead of leaving lexical search empty. After the marker is current, clean schema setup avoids repeated correlated scans over FTS virtual tables. It does not rewrite memory files, vectors, auth/config, provider state, or canonical chunk rows. Body-text FTS remains separate from path FTS, so filename lookup no longer changes body BM25 document-length normalization.

## Current review state

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- RF-001 / path FTS strategy choice: disclosed as a maintainer decision; this PR presents the separate derived path FTS table strategy on the canonical `memory_index_*` schema.
- RF-002 / persistent derived SQLite FTS table and repair-marker upgrade risk: addressed with atomic set-based repair, rollback coverage for failed repair, current-head CLI proof, and focused schema/search tests; the remaining upgrade-shape choice is still visible for maintainer review.
- RF-003 / sibling PR #94135 competing strategy: disclosed as a maintainer selection signal; this PR should be compared against that approach before merge.
- RF-004 / canonical memory FTS strategy and upgrade shape: disclosed as a maintainer decision; contributor-side proof and local safety checks are current.
- ClawSweeper commit review finding / FTS repair can drop lexical search state and spike memory on upgrade: fixed by replacing JS-materialized row-by-row repair with savepoint-protected set-based SQL rebuild, plus a regression that forces repair failure and verifies previous FTS rows remain available.

What is the next action?

Maintainers can compare this canonical-table approach with sibling PR #94135 and evaluate the normal remote checks on the current PR head.
target.author: zhangguiping-xydt

commands.reason: PR is not opted into ClawSweeper autofix or automerge
